quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 5 Quiz 10
quiz.subtitle: Tension and Normal Force in Circular Motion — Challenging
quiz.subject: Physics

questions.question: A $1,000\text{ kg}$ car crests a hill of radius $90\text{ m}$. At what speed will the car just lose contact with the road? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: At loss of contact, $F_N = 0$. $mg = mv^2/r$. $v = \sqrt{gr} = \sqrt{10 \times 90} = \sqrt{900} = 30\text{ m/s}$.

questions.question:
A $48.0\text{ kg}$ stunt performer swings from a high platform on a $12.5\text{ m}$ safety cable. As they pass through the lowest point of the arc, a sensor registers a linear speed of $5.40\text{ m/s}$.

Find the magnitude of the tension force stretching the cable at this point. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

A child swinging on a rope $6.00\text{ m}$ long passes through the lowest position with a speed of $4.00\text{ m/s}$. A load sensor records a peak tension of $434\text{ N}$ at this instant.

What is the mass of the child? ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

A mechanical testing rig swings a heavy block on a $4.50\text{ m}$ high-strength tether line. As the block glides through the lowest point at $6.20\text{ m/s}$, a strain gauge registers a tension of $1,580\text{ N}$.

Determine the mass of the test block. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

A $40.0\text{ kg}$ youth swings on a vine, passing through the lowest point at $3.50\text{ m/s}$. A tension meter reads a maximum force of $515\text{ N}$ at that moment.

What is the length of the swinging vine? ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

A $55.0\text{ kg}$ stunt coordinator attached to a high-tensile wire sweeps through the lowest point of an arc at $6.40\text{ m/s}$. The digital readout indicates a peak tension of $890\text{ N}$.

Calculate the radius of the circular path. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

A physics lab apparatus drops a $2.20\text{ kg}$ steel bob on a lightweight braided line. The bob passes through its lowest point at $4.10\text{ m/s}$, creating a peak tension of $36.5\text{ N}$.

Determine the length of the line. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

During a simulated mission on an artificial planetoid, a technician swings a $12.0\text{ kg}$ training pack on a rope $6.40\text{ m}$ long. At the lowest point, the pack moves at $5.50\text{ m/s}$ and a strain gauge records a maximum tension of $114\text{ N}$.

Calculate the simulated gravity field strength of the planetoid.

A robotic exploration rover on a dense moon uses a mechanical arm to swing a $0.750\text{ kg}$ package in a vertical loop. At the bottom of the loop ($r = 1.80\text{ m}$), the package travels at $2.10\text{ m/s}$ and creates a peak tension of $3.50\text{ N}$.

Find the moon's gravitational acceleration.

A fighter jet pulls out of a steep dive along a vertical circular arc whose center of curvature is $950\text{ m}$ directly above the aircraft. The jet maintains a constant speed of $185\text{ m/s}$ through the lowest point.

Calculate the upward force exerted by the seat on an $85.0\text{ kg}$ pilot. ($g = 9.80\text{ m/s}^2$)

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered tension and normal force calculations in circular motion!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review solving for different variables in $F_T = mg + mv^2/r$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on rearranging the equation to solve for mass, radius, or gravity.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: at the bottom of a vertical circle, $F_T = mg + mv^2/r$ and $F_N = mg + mv^2/r$.
